title: Sausage rolls
subtitle: Seasoned pork in a quick rough puff pastry case
excerpt: Twenty-four small sausage rolls made with a quick two-fold rough puff pastry and a filling of seasoned pork sausage meat with sage and mustard. The rough puff gives visible flaky layers; shop-bought all-butter puff delivers similar results if time is short.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the savoury pastry chapter documents seasoned sausage meat enclosed in puff or shortcrust pastry and baked until the pastry is risen and golden. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the savoury pastry section documents the technique of enclosing forcemeat in rolled pastry, with mustard and herbs as standard British seasonings. Sausage rolls in rough puff or puff pastry are documented in British domestic cookery from the mid-Victorian period; the recipe here is consistent with the public-domain literature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked assembled rolls on a tray, then transfer to a bag. Bake from frozen at 200°C fan, adding 8 minutes to the baking time.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well; make the pastry in two separate batches rather than one double quantity.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and refrigerate up to a day ahead, unbaked. The filling must be cold before the pastry goes on.

recipeTools.notes: A cold surface helps keep the butter in flakes during folding.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pressing the edge of a pastry seam with the tines of a fork, or pinching it between thumb and forefinger in a regular pattern, to seal the pastry closed and create a decorative border. A properly crimped edge stays sealed in the oven and prevents the filling from escaping. For sausage rolls, fork-crimping along the join line before cutting into portions is the most reliable method.

content.text: Sausage rolls stand or fall on the pastry. A rough puff made here takes an extra 30 minutes compared to opening a packet of ready-rolled, but produces visible flaky layers that separate in the oven where the butter has steamed. The filling needs only sage, a little mustard, and good seasoning — the pork sausage meat is the whole point.

attrs.title: Use cold filling
attrs.body: Spread the sausage meat onto a plate and refrigerate it while you make the pastry. Warm filling softens the pastry butter before it reaches the oven, which produces a greasy, un-flaky result. Everything that touches the pastry should be cold.

items.prepNote: small, finely grated and squeezed dry in a cloth
1 teaspoon — or 8 fresh sage leaves, finely chopped
season well — taste the raw mix before assembling

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} salt in a bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter dice and toss to coat in flour. Squeeze each piece of butter flat with your fingertips — you want flat, coin-sized pieces of butter, not fine breadcrumbs. Add the {{water}} ice cold water and cut through with a round-bladed knife until the dough just clumps. Press into a rough rectangle, wrap, and refrigerate for 15 minutes.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough into a rectangle about 30 × 20 cm. Fold the top third down and the bottom third up like a letter. Rotate 90°. Wrap and refrigerate for 15 minutes. Repeat once more: roll, fold, chill for 15 minutes. After two folds the dough should look streaky with visible layers.

Mix the {{sausage-meat}}, grated {{onion}}, {{sage-dried}}, {{english-mustard-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}} salt, and {{black-pepper-ground}} together in a bowl until combined. Pinch off a small piece, fry it in a dry pan, and taste for seasoning — sausage rolls need confident seasoning or they taste flat under all that pastry. Refrigerate the filling until the pastry is ready.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Roll the chilled pastry into a rectangle about 3–4 mm thick. Cut into two long strips, each about 12 cm wide. Divide the filling between the two strips, forming a log of filling down the centre of each, about 4 cm in diameter. Brush one long edge of each strip with {{eggs}} egg wash. Fold the pastry over to enclose the filling and press the join firmly.
the seam with a fork. Turn seam-side down and cut each log into 12 portions. Score the top of each with three diagonal cuts. Transfer to lined baking trays. Brush with egg wash.

Bake for 22–25 minutes, until the pastry is deep golden and the layers have opened visibly. The base should be crisp, not pale — check by lifting one. Serve warm or at room temperature. The pastry stays crisp for about 2 hours; after that it softens.
